notes.title: Wavefront
points: A wavefront is defined as the continuous locus of all particles of a medium which are vibrating in the same phase.
Spherical Wavefront: Produced by a point source.
Plane Wavefront: Produced by a source at infinity (small portion of a large sphere).
Cylindrical Wavefront: Produced by a linear source (like a slit).

notes.title: Types of Wavefronts
notes.caption: Rays are always perpendicular to the wavefronts.

notes.title: Huygens' Principle
points: Each point on a wavefront acts as a source of new disturbance, called secondary wavelets.
These wavelets spread out in all directions with the speed of the wave.
The new wavefront at any later time is given by the forward envelope (tangential surface) of these secondary wavelets.

notes.title: Refraction of a Plane Wave
assumptions: Plane wavefront incident on a plane surface separating two media.

result.direction: Snell's Law of Refraction derived from wave theory.

notes.title: Refraction by Huygens Principle
notes.caption: Geometry of wavefronts showing change in speed causes bending.

notes.title: Reflection of a Plane Wave
assumptions: Plane wavefront incident on a reflecting surface.

result.direction: Law of Reflection derived from wave theory.

notes.title: Coherent Sources
points: Two sources are said to be coherent if they emit light waves of the same frequency and a constant phase difference.
Interference patterns are observed only with coherent sources.
Two independent sources (e.g., two bulbs) are incoherent and cannot produce a sustained interference pattern.

notes.title: Interference of Light
points: The phenomenon of redistribution of light energy due to the superposition of light waves from two coherent sources.
Constructive Interference: Waves meet in phase (Maximum intensity).
Destructive Interference: Waves meet out of phase (Minimum intensity).

notes.title: Young's Double Slit Experiment (YDSE)
points: Demonstrated interference of light.
Wavefront from a single source S is split into two coherent sources S1 and S2.

notes.title: YDSE Setup
notes.caption: S1 and S2 act as coherent sources separated by distance d; Screen at distance D.

notes.label: Resultant Intensity
conditions: phi is the phase difference between the two waves.
If I1 = I2 = I0, then I = 4 I0 cos^2(phi/2).

notes.label: Path Difference in YDSE
conditions: x is the distance of point on screen from central maximum.

notes.label: Fringe Width
conditions: Distance between two consecutive bright or dark fringes.
Depends on wavelength, screen distance, and slit separation.

notes.title: Interference Intensity Distribution
notes.caption: All bright fringes have equal intensity (4I0).

notes.title: Diffraction
points: The bending of light around the corners of an obstacle or aperture.
Observable when the size of the obstacle is comparable to the wavelength of light.
Single Slit Diffraction: Produces a broad central maximum and weaker secondary maxima.

notes.title: Single Slit Diffraction
notes.caption: Slit width 'a'. Path difference between edges is a*sin(theta).

notes.valid_when: Note: Condition is 'reversed' compared to interference maxima.
Intensity of secondary maxima decreases rapidly.

notes.label: Width of Central Maximum
conditions: Twice the width of secondary fringes.
Spreads out if slit width 'a' is decreased.

notes.title: Diffraction vs Interference Graph
notes.caption: Interference: Equal width/intensity. Diffraction: Central max dominates.

notes.title: Polarisation
points: Property of transverse waves specifying the geometrical orientation of the oscillations.
Light is a transverse EM wave.
Unpolarised light: Electric vector oscillates in all directions perpendicular to propagation.

notes.label: Malus's Law
conditions: I is intensity of polarised light transmitted through an analyser.
theta is angle between pass axes of polariser and analyser.

notes.title: Polarisation by Reflection (Brewster's Law)
points: When unpolarised light is incident at a specific angle (Brewster's angle), the reflected light is completely polarised.
At this angle, the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other.

notes.label: Brewster's Law
conditions: ip is the polarising angle (Brewster's angle).
n is the refractive index of the reflecting medium.

notes.title: Brewster's Angle
notes.caption: Reflected ray (dots) is perpendicular to refracted ray.

ncert_golden_lines: A wavefront is the locus of points having the same phase of oscillation.
According to Huygens principle, each point of the wavefront is the source of a secondary disturbance.
Interference is the phenomenon in which two waves superpose to form a resultant wave of lower, higher or same amplitude.
For constructive interference, the path difference must be an integral multiple of the wavelength.
Diffraction is a general characteristic exhibited by all types of waves, be it sound waves, light waves, water waves or matter waves.
In interference, all bright fringes have the same intensity. In diffraction, the central maximum is the brightest.
Light waves are transverse in nature; this is proved by the phenomenon of polarisation.

diagram_descriptors.detailed_description: Rays are always perpendicular to the wavefronts.
Geometry of wavefronts showing change in speed causes bending.
S1 and S2 act as coherent sources separated by distance d; Screen at distance D.
All bright fringes have equal intensity (4I0).
Slit width 'a'. Path difference between edges is a*sin(theta).
Interference: Equal width/intensity. Diffraction: Central max dominates.
Reflected ray (dots) is perpendicular to refracted ray.
